senses.definition: Having skin that is bound to or covered by something, or tightly connected to the skin.
examples: The skinbound nature of the plant fibers makes the fabric stiff.
In the medical report, the wound remained skinbound to the surrounding tissue.

senses.definition: The activities and products used to care for the skin to keep it healthy and clean.
examples: She follows a daily skincare routine.
Dermatologists recommended a skincare regimen with sunscreen.

senses.definition: Plural form of skincare; multiple skincare routines or products.
examples: The shop offers several skincares tailored to different skin types.
They reviewed several skincares before choosing one.

senses.definition: The skin of an animal or person; also a term used in some dialects for skin or hide.
examples: The leather came from the skinch of the animal.
They peeled the skinch off the fruit.

senses.definition: To dive or swim while keeping skin close to or exposed near water, often a playful or exploratory dive.
examples: They decided to skindive into the calm lake at dawn.
Children skindived from the pier.

senses.definition: A person who skdives or dives with skin exposed; often used playfully for someone who enjoys diving from shore or boat.
examples: The family rented gear for a day of skindivers at the cove.
As a skindiver, he loved exploring shallow reefs.

senses.definition: The activity of diving with skin exposed, typically in shallow water for recreation or exploration.
examples: Skindiving is popular in tropical coastal areas.
They spent the morning skindiving among coral formations.

senses.definition: A light, quick movement of the skin, such as a flicking motion with the hand; can also be a playful reference to a skin flick graphic or video.
examples: The dog gave a skinflick of its tail.
A humorous skinflick appeared in the video montage.

senses.definition: Films or videos that emphasize skin or provocative content; used colloquially for risqué entertainment.
examples: The shop sold vintage skinc flicks.
He watched a few skinc flicks online.

senses.definition: A person who does not like to spend money; very cheap.
examples: He's such a skinflint that he saves every penny.
Don't be a skinflint—buy the nicer one.

senses.definition: In a stingy or miserly way.
examples: He skinflintily refused to lend any money.
She skinflintily paid the bill, avoiding tips.

senses.definition: Quality of being stingy or very cheap.
examples: Her skinflintiness surprised everyone at the dinner party.
A culture of skinflintiness dominated the office budget.

senses.definition: People who are stingy or very cheap.
examples: The group of skinflints refused to split the bill.
Skinflints among the team saved all year.

senses.definition: Being stingy or cheap; characteristic of a skinflint.
examples: His skinflinty habits annoyed his friends.
A skinflinty approach to expenses ruined the trip.

senses.definition: Food that is good for the skin; often marketed for skin health.
examples: This smoothie is marketed as skinfood.
Some people take vitamins as skinfood for clearer skin.

senses.definition: Various foods believed to benefit skin health.
examples: They stocked a range of skinfoods at the wellness shop.
Skinfoods are often rich in vitamins A and C.

senses.definition: A large amount of skin; used humorously to describe a lot of something.
examples: We had a skinful of sun during the afternoon.
He drank a skinful and laughed loudly.

senses.definition: Multiple large amounts of skin or, more commonly, large amounts of something quantified informally.
examples: They carried skinfuls of equipment to the campsite.
Two skinfuls of ale made him sleepy.

senses.definition: A person with a shaved head, often associated with a subculture; can be used neutrally or pejoratively depending on context.
examples: A skinhead stood at the bus stop.
The skinhead subculture has various styles.

senses.definition: Multiple people who have shaved heads; a group identity.
examples: The skinheads in the parade wore boots and braces.
Several skinheads gathered near the station.

senses.definition: A type of lizard or the flesh of a skink; also a dish in some regions.
examples: The skink basked on a rock.
In some cuisines, skink is prepared as a soup.

senses.definition: Past tense of skink; to have become a skink or to have had skin removed in some context.
examples: The leather was skinked to remove imperfections.
The old statue was skinked during restoration.

senses.definition: A person who skins or processes hides; an old occupation term.
examples: The skinker prepared hides for leatherwork.
Historical texts mention skiners and skinkers.

senses.definition: Multiple skinders or skinkers; people who skin hides.
examples: The guild of skinkers met weekly.
Skinkers traded hides for leather goods.

senses.definition: The act of skinning or the process of removing skin; also a term in fish processing.
examples: Skinking was the first step in preparing the leather.
The fish underwent skinking before curing.

senses.definition: A small, smooth lizard with shiny skin; commonly found in warm regions.
examples: A skink darted across the patio.
Researchers studied the skink's habitat.

senses.definition: Past tense of skink; to have become a skink or to have been skinned in some context.
examples: The lizard was skinked by the predator.
The armor was skinked during battle.

senses.definition: A person who skins or processes hides; an old occupational term.
examples: The skinker prepared the hides for tanning.
Historical accounts mention skinkers in early markets.

senses.definition: Multiple skinkers; people who skin hides.
examples: The guild of skinkers met monthly.
Skinkers traded leather at the market.

senses.definition: The act of skinning or the process of removing skin; in some contexts, part of leather preparation.
examples: Skinking is the initial step in preparing pale leather.
The workshop described the skinking stage in detail.
